target.title: fix(podman): mount host ~/.claude into setup container
target.body:
## Summary
- Setup wizard fails with `Claude CLI is not authenticated on this host` under rootless Podman because `~/.claude` is never mounted into the onboard container, so `readClaudeCliCredentials` cannot find `/home/node/.claude/.credentials.json`.
- `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` is intentionally stripped by `CLAUDE_CLI_CLEAR_ENV` (`extensions/anthropic/cli-shared.ts`), so the env workaround does not survive managed runs.
- Bind-mount the host's `~/.claude` read-only into the **setup container only** when `.credentials.json` exists. Gateway and other non-setup launch paths are untouched.
- Reuse the existing `ensure_private_existing_dir_owned_by_user` + `ensure_private_existing_regular_file_owned_by_user` guards on both `$CLAUDE_HOST_DIR` and `$CLAUDE_HOST_DIR/.credentials.json` before mounting — rejects symlinks, wrong owner, and group/other-writable sources.
- **Soft-fail**: when guards reject the source, emit a warning and skip the mount; setup still proceeds so users configuring a non-Claude provider, or those with no `~/.claude` at all, are never blocked by Claude credential hygiene.
- Use the shared SELinux relabel (`,z`) instead of the exclusive `,Z` so the host's own Claude CLI keeps access to its credentials after the setup container exits.
- Allow overriding the host source via `OPENCLAW_HOST_CLAUDE_DIR`.

Fixes #81899

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Rootless Podman setup wizard run from a host that has a working `~/.claude/.credentials.json` fails at "Anthropic Claude CLI" onboarding with `Claude CLI is not authenticated on this host` (#81899), because `scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh` never mounted the host `.claude` directory into the setup container. After this patch the setup container gets a conditional, read-only, shared-SELinux-relabel bind mount of the host `~/.claude`, soft-gated on private-owner + symlink + permission checks, scoped to `RUN_SETUP == true` so non-setup gateway launches are unaffected, with the validation failing to a warning rather than aborting setup.

- **Real environment tested:** Fresh Fedora 43 Cloud Edition VM (DigitalOcean droplet, redacted IP), SELinux **Enforcing**, kernel `6.17.1-300.fc43.x86_64`, `podman 5.6.2`, rootless non-root `tester` user (uid 1000) with linger enabled, real `~/.claude/.credentials.json` (mode 600, owner tester), patched openclaw checkout at HEAD `5541bad4`. No mocks of the production validation helpers; only `podman` was wrapped for argv capture in the non-real-podman steps. The real-mount step uses upstream `registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-minimal` to confirm the SELinux `,z` relabel.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `git fetch && git checkout 5541bad4` on the Fedora droplet as `tester`
  2. `bash -n scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh`
  3. Positive setup with `~/.claude` perms 700/600 owned by tester — assert mount argv emitted, no warning
  4. Setup compatibility (P1 fix): chmod 640 file / chmod 770 dir / symlink file / symlink dir — assert warning printed AND setup `podman run` still invoked (exit 0, mount absent)
  5. Non-setup `launch` with loose perms — assert exit 0, gateway container starts
  6. Real container: `podman run --rm -v $HOME/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora-minimal /bin/bash -c "[ -r /home/node/.claude/.credentials.json ]"`
  7. Post-exit host check: `claude auth status` on the host

- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live terminal output from the live Fedora 43 rootless Podman + SELinux **Enforcing** run on commit `5541bad4`. Token, email, and org UUID redacted in-place; hostname and IP redacted.

```
=== ENV (soft-fail commit 5541bad4) ===

Fedora Linux 43 (Cloud Edition) / SELinux: Enforcing / podman 5.6.2
openclaw SHA: 5541bad46f0d3354ad502d21701486005ed2519a

=== HAPPY PATH — perms 700/600 owner=tester — mount EMITTED, no warning ===

700 tester:tester /home/tester/.claude
600 tester:tester /home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json

==podman-invoked==
argc=30
  run
  --pull=never
  --rm
  -it
  --init
  --userns=keep-id
  --user
  1000:1000
  -e
  HOME=/home/node
  -e
  TERM=xterm-256color
  -e
  BROWSER=echo
  -e
  NPM_CONFIG_CACHE=/home/node/.openclaw/.npm
  -e
  OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1
  --env-file
  /home/tester/.openclaw/.token.env.c3IZUX
  -v
  /home/tester/.openclaw:/home/node/.openclaw:rw,Z
  -v
  /home/tester/.openclaw/workspace:/home/node/.openclaw/workspace:rw,Z
  -v
  /home/tester/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z
  openclaw:local
  node
  dist/index.js
  onboard
```

```
=== STEP 3 (P1 fix) — setup + chmod 640 creds — WARN, skip mount, setup CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials file: expected owner-only permissions (/home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
exit=1 podman invocations (must be 1)

=== STEP 4 (P1 fix) — setup + chmod 770 dir — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials directory: group/other writable (/home/tester/.claude)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 5 (P1 fix) — setup + symlinked .credentials.json — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials file: symlinks are not allowed (/home/tester/.claude/.credentials.json)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 6 (P1 fix) — setup + symlinked dir — WARN, skip, CONTINUES ===

warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.
         Unsafe claude credentials directory: symlinks are not allowed (/home/tester/.claude)
         Non-Claude providers will still onboard; for Anthropic Claude CLI, fix permissions/ownership of /home/tester/.claude and re-run setup.
==podman-invoked==
podman invocations: 1

=== STEP 7 — NON-setup (gateway) + loose creds (644) — no abort, no mount, exit 0 ===

Container openclaw started: http://127.0.0.1:18789/
podman exec -it openclaw openclaw dashboard --no-open
podman exec -it openclaw openclaw devices approve --latest  # if pairing required
podman logs -f openclaw
For auto-start/restarts, use: ./scripts/podman/setup.sh --quadlet (Quadlet + systemd user service).

=== STEP 8 — setup happy path POST-recovery — mount re-emitted ===


=== STEP 9 — real podman ,z mount — file readable in container ===

time="2026-05-18T20:56:01Z" level=warning msg="Alternatively, you can enable lingering with: `loginctl enable-linger 1000` (possibly as root)"
time="2026-05-18T20:56:01Z" level=warning msg="Falling back to --cgroup-manager=cgroupfs"
READABLE=yes

=== STEP 10 — host claude auth status post-container ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:**
  - **Setup happy path** (dir 700, file 600, owner=tester): `-v /home/tester/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z` emitted in the podman argv exactly once, no warning.
  - **Setup P1 compatibility fix** (unsafe credentials no longer abort setup): each of `file 640`, `dir 770`, `file is symlink`, `dir is symlink` prints `warning: skipping host Claude credentials mount; setup will continue without it.` plus the exact private-check reason (`"expected owner-only permissions"`, `"group/other writable"`, `"symlinks are not allowed"`), and setup still invokes `podman run` (mock-podman shows exactly 1 invocation per case with the `.claude` mount absent). The prior review's `[P1] Preserve setup when Claude creds are unsafe — scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh:537-539` is closed: unsafe credentials no longer kill setup; non-Claude provider selection in the onboarding TUI remains reachable.
  - **Non-setup compatibility**: with loose file perms (644), symlinked `.claude`, and missing `.credentials.json` the gateway launch path completes with exit code 0; no validation abort and no `.claude` mount appears in the gateway podman argv — the prior review's `[P1] Keep Claude credential validation inside setup` stays closed.
  - **Real `podman run` with the PR's `,z` mount**: container reads `/home/node/.claude/.credentials.json` (`READABLE=yes`).
  - **Host `claude auth status` after container exit** reports `loggedIn: true`, confirming the host's own Claude CLI continues to authenticate (load-bearing for `,z` shared relabel vs `,Z` exclusive).

- **What was not tested:** End-to-end onboarding TUI through the production `openclaw` container image was not exercised because the proof above already covers the load-bearing wiring (script-level mount construction, soft-fail behavior, real SELinux relabel against a real `podman run`, post-exit host auth). A maintainer with a Fedora rootless Podman host can run `./scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh launch setup`, pick a provider inside the wizard, and confirm the end-to-end happy path; the diff is a small shell-script patch with no schema or migration risk.

## Test plan
- [x] `bash -n scripts/run-openclaw-podman.sh`
- [x] Live Fedora 43 rootless Podman + SELinux enforcing setup happy path — mount argv emitted
- [x] Live Fedora 43 setup with unsafe creds (P1 fix) — warning emitted, setup continues, mount absent
- [x] Live Fedora 43 non-setup gateway path with loose perms / symlink / missing creds — no abort, no mount
- [x] Real `podman run -v $HOME/.claude:/home/node/.claude:ro,z` — container reads credentials file
- [x] Host `claude auth status` after container exit — still logged in (proves `,z` shared relabel, not `,Z` exclusive)

## AI-assisted disclosure
- [x] AI-assisted (Claude Code, Opus 4.7).
- [x] I understand what the code does.
- [x] Human-run live proof on a real Fedora SELinux-enforcing rootless Podman host is included above.

commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
Adds an opt-in Anthropic `claude-cli-interactive` backend that runs Claude Code through a Bun loopback TLS proxy, reconstructs stream-json events, extends Claude CLI routing/auth gates, packages proxy assets, and adds focused tests.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review blockers by source inspection. A custom `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` still leaves proxy keys at `homedir()/.openclaw`, and over-cap prompts spill to stdin before the wrapper asks Claude to use a `Read` tool that is not default-allowed by this backend.
commands.review_followup: This needs author changes plus an explicit maintainer provider/security-boundary decision, so it is not a safe autonomous repair lane yet.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched (shared gate functions — model-capability resolution, OAuth label, credential fingerprint, live-agent probes, model alias detection — extended via a single `isClaudeCliCompatibleBackend(provider)` helper to recognise both backend ids; every gate continues to return identical values for `claude-cli`). `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

commands.review_summary:
The PR changes `/models` auto-reply browsing to load the model catalog read-only by default, disables runtime auth discovery for that static visibility pass, and keeps explicit `all` browse on the full catalog path.

target.title: Speed up /models browse replies
target.body:
## Summary

- use the read-only model catalog for default `/models` browse replies
- disable runtime auth discovery for that static browse visibility pass, matching the fast Gateway-style behavior
- keep explicit `/models ... all` on the full catalog path

Fixes #84731.

## Local verification

In a LiteLLM-backed environment with about 410 configured models, runtime patch testing reduced the auto-reply command timings from roughly:

- `/models`: ~69s -> ~1.3s
- `/models litellm`: ~28-32s -> ~1.3s
- `/models list litellm`: ~32s -> ~1.3s

Explicit `all` remains on the full path and stayed slow by design.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts`

target.title: Doctor: convert interactive maintenance checks
target.body:
## Goal

Start the repair-side migration for structured `openclaw doctor` checks with a small set of interactive maintenance repairs.

The structured health-check contract already gives doctor a read-only detection shape:

```ts
detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[]
repair(ctx, findings) -> HealthRepairResult
```

This PR is where the repair half starts doing real work. It applies that shape to repairs that prompt, mutate state, report effects, support dry-run previews, and need post-repair validation. The walkthroughs here tighten the contract future repair conversions should follow: `detect()` remains the read-only source of truth; `repair()` receives the exact findings it may fix; repair output is machine-useful through `changes`, `warnings`, `diffs`, and `effects`.

The broader repair wave uses that same output shape so doctor can eventually show dry-run plans, diffs, and effects consistently for repairable findings. This base PR does not turn that richer reporting into the default user surface by itself; the follow-on chain at the end converts the remaining repair buckets first so the feature can be enabled once the migrated majority has the same contract.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, no runner note arrays.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Legacy interactive doctor code decided what to find, what to ask, what to mutate, and what to print inside local flows. This PR starts moving that behavior into an isomorphic health-check shape: the same `detect()` result can power lint, repair selection, dry-run reporting, and post-repair validation. The repair path remains interactive where it already was interactive, but the output is now structured enough for later preview and automation surfaces.

## Converted Here

This PR migrates the first interactive maintenance checks into structured doctor repairs:

- MCP invalid entry removal
- MCP stale config repair
- MCP safe config cleanup
- MCP API migration
- legacy project migration
- ambiguous agent path detection
- invalid agent metadata detection
- orphan agent cleanup

These are useful base cases because they cover prompts, filesystem mutation, dry-run previews, and follow-up validation without reaching into plugin-specific or provider-specific policy.

## Review Surface

This is the contract slice to challenge before the rest of the stack builds on it:

- whether `repair(ctx, findings)` is the right repair boundary for converted doctor checks
- whether detect-after validation should run after every successful non-dry-run repair
- whether `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs` are the right machine-readable repair vocabulary
- whether notes should stay purely presentation-only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`

## Follow-On Stack

This is the base PR for the current doctor repair stack:

| PR | Area |
| --- | --- |
| #84290 | plugin registry repairs |
| #84326 | sandbox repairs |
| #84340 | gateway service repairs |
| #84366 | session state repairs |
| #84450 | cron/config audit repairs |
| #84472 | dry-run preview reports |

Those PRs should build on this contract instead of reintroducing per-flow repair notes, ad hoc lint output, or local presentation models.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts`
- Existing CI should provide broader validation for the full stack after the refreshed single-commit PRs are replayed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: This PR starts the structured Doctor repair migration by applying finding-scoped repair, machine-readable repair results, presentation-only notes through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`, and detect-after validation to the first interactive maintenance repairs.

Real environment tested: Windows checkout at commit `9c35ac54686c0e3c4448208f3f448235e6ae2af6`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: 5 focused Doctor test files passed, 55 tests total, covering structured repair execution, repair filtering, positional repair ordering, dry-run behavior, diff propagation, presentation-only notes, and detect-after repair validation.

Observed result after fix: The converted repair paths expose findings and repair outputs only. Human notes flow through the existing suppressible note system when a doctor runner provides `ctx.doctor.note(...)`. Lint remains findings-only. Positional core repairs do not re-register bundled health checks while moving through the Doctor contribution order.

What was not tested: Full doctor-stack CI and platform-wide doctor scenarios were not rerun locally for this body refresh; those should run through normal PR CI across the stack.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache provider-normalized tool schema parameters for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable normalization-hook identity.
- Keep the cache conservative and hook-owned: key includes the provider hook identity plus provider/model/API/base URL and ordered tool names with stable original schema signatures; values store only cloned `parameters`, never tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, with cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, and eviction coverage.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, `execute` closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/guanbear/workspace/openclaw-5.4-src`, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, head `a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e`, macOS host, Node 25.9.0 via repo `pnpm` tooling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `pnpm exec tsx --input-type=module` locally with a script that imports `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, uses the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path, recreates 50 representative OpenAI-style tool objects and fresh `execute` closures per iteration, then runs 100 normalization iterations with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` and 100 iterations with the default cache enabled. Also ran a same-Gateway-process mock OpenAI E2E before the rebase with five main-agent RPC calls per mode and 27 tools sent per model request.
- **Evidence after fix:** current-head terminal output from the provider schema-normalization microbenchmark:

```json
{
  "head": "a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e",
  "cases": [
    {
      "label": "cache-disabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 63625,
      "perIterationMs": 636.25,
      "rssDeltaMb": 447.7,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 100,
        "hit": 0,
        "miss": 0,
        "store": 0,
        "size": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "cache-enabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 26927,
      "perIterationMs": 269.27,
      "rssDeltaMb": -3.1,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 0,
        "hit": 99,
        "miss": 1,
        "store": 1,
        "size": 1
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Additional lightweight mock Gateway E2E evidence from the same PR code before the rebase:

- cache enabled durations `[2105, 496, 471, 465, 470]`, p50 `471ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request
- cache disabled durations `[2002, 482, 472, 474, 470]`, p50 `474ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request

- **Observed result after fix:** the provider schema-normalization cache is active on the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path: the benchmark stores once, then records 99 cache hits while recreating tool objects and closures every iteration. The current conservative implementation reduces the repeated normalization path from `636.25ms` to `269.27ms` per iteration in this 50-tool fixture and cuts RSS growth, but the hit path still pays cache-key and original-schema-signature construction cost. The mock Gateway E2E confirms the real main-agent path still succeeds with the cache enabled, but it does not show a meaningful wall-latency delta in a five-run mocked fixture.
- **What was not tested:** full live-provider end-to-end agent reply latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache full tool inventory/materialization, prompt-report inputs, `core-plugin-tools`, policy resolution, or complete agent-turn startup. The microbenchmark should not be read as a 400ms-per-turn full agent latency claim.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Current-head scoped `pnpm exec tsx` provider schema-normalization microbenchmark described above
- `git diff --check`

target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- expose existing embedded runner prep stage snapshots on attempt results and run meta
- include prep stage timings on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability
- add coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation
- document the new optional hook field and add an Unreleased changelog entry

## Motivation
OpenClaw already records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not currently available to runtime metadata consumers or plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without patching core locally.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a stable, low-risk way to collect real latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations such as tool bundle filtering or prompt caching. This keeps future performance work data-driven instead of relying on coarse end-to-end timings.

## Implementation notes
- reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path
- keeps all new fields optional
- uses a public structural plugin hook type instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API
- does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior
- documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are available to runtime metadata consumers and `agent_end` hook observers without changing runner behavior.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw source checkout at `/Users/guanbear/.config/superpowers/worktrees/OctoClaw/resolve-pr-78381`, branch `prep-metrics-pr1`, current head `fef1a64765dd57ced7e90a4fa24969e5f1d37eb9`. The proof used a temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`; a real `openclaw agent --local` embedded run; the repository OpenAI-compatible local response server; and a temporary non-bundled plugin registered through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
MOCK_PORT=41xxx SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK \
  MOCK_REQUEST_LOG=/tmp/openclaw-pr78381-proof-JCgOKx/mock-openai.log \
  node scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs

node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect prep-proof --runtime --json

node scripts/run-node.mjs agent \
  --local \
  --agent main \
  --session-id prep-proof-session \
  --message "Reply exactly OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK" \
  --thinking off \
  --json
```

- Evidence after fix: Terminal capture from the current-head runtime proof:

```json
{
  "head": "fef1a64765dd57ced7e90a4fa24969e5f1d37eb9",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "metaHasPrepStages": true,
  "metaPrepStages": {
    "totalMs": 1293,
    "stages": [
      { "name": "workspace-sandbox", "durationMs": 1, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "skills", "durationMs": 0, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "core-plugin-tools", "durationMs": 206, "elapsedMs": 207 },
      { "name": "bootstrap-context", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 209 },
      { "name": "bundle-tools", "durationMs": 151, "elapsedMs": 360 },
      { "name": "system-prompt", "durationMs": 850, "elapsedMs": 1210 },
      { "name": "session-resource-loader", "durationMs": 62, "elapsedMs": 1272 },
      { "name": "agent-session", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 1274 },
      { "name": "stream-setup", "durationMs": 19, "elapsedMs": 1293 }
    ]
  },
  "hookProof": {
    "success": true,
    "durationMs": 607,
    "hasPrepStages": true,
    "totalMs": 1293,
    "stages": [
      { "name": "workspace-sandbox", "durationMs": 1, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "skills", "durationMs": 0, "elapsedMs": 1 },
      { "name": "core-plugin-tools", "durationMs": 206, "elapsedMs": 207 },
      { "name": "bootstrap-context", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 209 },
      { "name": "bundle-tools", "durationMs": 151, "elapsedMs": 360 },
      { "name": "system-prompt", "durationMs": 850, "elapsedMs": 1210 },
      { "name": "session-resource-loader", "durationMs": 62, "elapsedMs": 1272 },
      { "name": "agent-session", "durationMs": 2, "elapsedMs": 1274 },
      { "name": "stream-setup", "durationMs": 19, "elapsedMs": 1293 }
    ]
  },
  "proofLog": "/tmp/openclaw-pr78381-proof-JCgOKx/agent-end-prepStages.jsonl"
}
```

The temporary plugin's raw `agent_end` log line from the same run had `hasPrepStages: true`, `prepStages.totalMs: 1293`, and stage entries for `workspace-sandbox`, `skills`, `core-plugin-tools`, `bootstrap-context`, `bundle-tools`, `system-prompt`, `session-resource-loader`, `agent-session`, and `stream-setup`. The agent command JSON from the same run also included `meta.prepStages` and `finalAssistantVisibleText: "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK"`, confirming both runtime metadata and the plugin hook observer saw prep-stage telemetry on current head.

- Observed result after fix: A standalone `openclaw agent --local` embedded run completed successfully against the local OpenAI-compatible response server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary real plugin's `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with `bundle-tools`, `system-prompt`, and `stream-setup` entries.
- What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run; the standalone proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-stage-timing.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.phase-hooks.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.model-override-wiring.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` -> lanes `core`, `coreTests`, `docs`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix(doctor): warn when sandbox hides MCP tools
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: sandboxed agents could hide configured MCP server tools even though the MCP servers loaded successfully, and there was no clear `openclaw doctor` warning for the missing sandbox tool allow gate.
- Solution: add a doctor warning when `mcp.servers` are configured but active sandbox tool policy does not allow `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins`, or a matching server glob such as `outlook__*`.
- What changed: mirror runtime sandbox policy fallback for per-agent partial overrides, preserve warnings when a separate agent intentionally denies MCP, polish warning grammar/source labels, and document sanitized server globs plus the current bundled-plugin/Claude `.mcp.json` diagnostic limitation.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no sandbox defaults, provider serialization, MCP runtime behavior, runtime tool policy enforcement, or bundled-plugin/`.mcp.json` MCP discovery logic changed.

## Motivation

- Related to #80909: the reporter confirmed adding `"bundle-mcp"` to `tools.sandbox.tools.alsoAllow` made the real MCP tools visible again in sandboxed turns.
- The failure mode is confusing because the MCP server can load successfully while the sandbox tool policy filters the provider-visible tool list before the request.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80909
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Sandboxed OpenClaw turns with configured `mcp.servers` now surface a doctor warning when the active sandbox tool allow gate would hide bundled MCP tools before provider requests.

- Real environment tested:

Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/80909-sandbox-mcp-docs`, rebased onto current `upstream/main`, using a temp state dir at `/tmp/openclaw-80909-doctor-state` and running the real `pnpm openclaw doctor` CLI.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-80909-doctor-state pnpm openclaw doctor --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
◇  Doctor warnings ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                         │
│  - mcp.servers defines 1 MCP server ("outlook"), but                    │
│    tools.sandbox.tools.alsoAllow does not include "bundle-mcp",         │
│    "group:plugins", or a matching "<server>__*" MCP tool pattern.       │
│    Sandboxed agents will filter bundled MCP tools before provider       │
│    requests. Add "bundle-mcp" to tools.sandbox.tools.alsoAllow (or use  │
│    "group:plugins" / server globs) if those MCP tools should be         │
│    visible; use tools.sandbox.tools.allow: [] only when you             │
│    intentionally want no sandbox allow gate.                            │
│                                                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

The command exited `0`. The run also printed unrelated local temp-state doctor findings such as missing UI assets, gateway auth/connect noise, and existing build warnings from bundled zod locale `.d.cts` files.

- Observed result after fix:

`openclaw doctor` prints the targeted sandbox/MCP warning in the Doctor warnings panel instead of silently allowing the misconfiguration to pass.

- What was not tested:

An end-to-end sandboxed provider turn against a live MCP server was not rerun in this checkout; the reporter's real setup already confirmed `"bundle-mcp"` fixes the missing provider-visible MCP tools. This PR also intentionally does not make doctor enumerate MCP servers loaded from bundled plugin manifests or Claude `.mcp.json`; the docs call that limitation out.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

Before the first fix commit, `/tmp/openclaw-80909-red-check.mjs` returned `[]` for the target config and failed because it expected the sandbox/MCP warning.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: sandbox tool policy is a second allow gate for sandboxed sessions. Configured MCP servers can load under `bundle-mcp`, but if `tools.sandbox.tools` lacks `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins`, or a matching `<server>__*` pattern, those MCP tools are filtered before provider requests.
- Missing detection / guardrail: `openclaw doctor` did not flag the configuration shape, so users could diagnose it as a provider serialization or MCP loading bug.
- Contributing context: per-agent sandbox tool overrides fall back field-by-field at runtime, so the diagnostic must mirror that behavior to avoid false positives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/doctor/shared/plugin-tool-allowlist-warnings.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: sandboxed `mcp.servers` warn when the active sandbox allow gate omits MCP/plugin entries, do not warn for `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins`, server globs, or explicit allow-all, and do not false-positive on per-agent partial override fallback.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the warning helper owns the exact doctor warning logic and can exercise the sandbox policy combinations without a full gateway runtime.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for the new sandbox/MCP warning path before this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor` can now warn when sandbox policy is likely hiding configured MCP server tools from sandboxed agents. Docs now clarify which sandbox allow entries expose MCP/plugin tools, how sanitized server globs are formed, and that bundled-plugin/Claude `.mcp.json` MCP sources use the same gate but are not enumerated by this diagnostic yet.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
mcp.servers loads -> sandboxed session tool gate omits bundle-mcp -> provider sees no MCP tools -> doctor has no targeted warning

After:
mcp.servers loads -> doctor compares active sandbox allow policy -> warning explains bundle-mcp / group:plugins / server glob fix
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux container/dev host
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw checkout with pnpm/node toolchain
- Model/provider: N/A for doctor helper/unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): configured MCP server shape from #80909
- Relevant config (redacted): temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-80909-doctor-state` with sandbox mode enabled and `mcp.servers.outlook` configured

### Steps

1. Run focused warning helper unit tests.
2. Run the original red repro script and verify it emits the target warning.
3. Run the fallback repro script and verify it emits `[]`.
4. Run TS/docs format checks and docs MDX validation.
5. Run real `openclaw doctor` against the temp state dir and inspect the Doctor warnings panel.

### Expected

- Missing sandbox MCP allow entries produce the targeted warning.
- Explicit MCP allow entries and runtime fallback-compatible configs do not warn.
- Docs and formatting checks pass.

### Actual

- `pnpm test:unit:fast -- src/commands/doctor/shared/plugin-tool-allowlist-warnings.test.ts` -> 19 tests passed.
- `node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-80909-red-check.mjs` -> emitted the target warning.
- `node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-80909-agent-fallback-red.mjs` -> printed `[]`.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor/shared/plugin-tool-allowlist-warnings.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/plugin-tool-allowlist-warnings.test.ts` -> all matched files formatted.
- `pnpm format:docs:check -- docs/gateway/config-tools.md docs/gateway/sandbox-vs-tool-policy-vs-elevated.md` -> docs formatting clean.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` -> passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` -> passed.
- `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-80909-doctor-state pnpm openclaw doctor --non-interactive --no-workspace-suggestions` -> printed the target Doctor warnings panel and exited `0`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: missing sandbox MCP allow entry warning, `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins`, server glob, explicit allow-all, sandbox off, per-agent fallback inheritance, intentional deny preserving global warning, sanitized server-name glob.
- Edge cases checked: multi-source warning grammar, unset sandbox allowlist source label, sanitized `mcp.servers["Outlook Graph"]` prefix, docs for bundled-plugin/Claude `.mcp.json` diagnostic limitation.
- What I did **not** verify: end-to-end sandboxed provider turn against a live MCP server after this local patch; bundled-plugin manifest or `.mcp.json` MCP discovery expansion, intentionally out of scope.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: doctor warning could false-positive if it diverges from runtime sandbox tool policy fallback.
  - Mitigation: helper now mirrors `allow`, `alsoAllow`, and `deny` fallback field-by-field, with focused regression tests for partial overrides and intentional deny behavior.
- Risk: users with server names containing spaces/non-provider-safe characters could use the raw config key in a server glob.
  - Mitigation: docs now state server globs use the sanitized provider-safe server prefix and tests cover `"Outlook Graph"` -> `outlook-graph__*`.
- Risk: users might expect this diagnostic to cover all MCP sources.
  - Mitigation: docs explicitly state bundled-plugin manifests and Claude `.mcp.json` MCP servers use the same sandbox gate but are not enumerated by this diagnostic yet.

## AI assistance disclosure

This patch and PR draft were prepared with AI assistance and locally verified before submission.

target.title: Warn on plaintext secret config in doctor
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a `doctor` security warning when `openclaw.json` contains plaintext secret-bearing fields such as `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` and sensitive provider headers like `Authorization`.
- Skip the warning for SecretRef-backed values so migrated configs stay quiet.
- Point users to `openclaw secrets configure` / `openclaw secrets apply` and `openclaw secrets audit --check`.

## Why

Issue #11829 tracks API key exposure to agents. The current secrets tooling can already audit and migrate plaintext values, but a normal `openclaw doctor` run can still look healthy while `openclaw.json` stores agent-readable API keys/tokens. This change makes that risk visible in the security warning surface users already run.

This does not claim to replace the broader secret-isolation architecture. It closes a practical visibility gap and helps users migrate away from plaintext config secrets before agents or workspace tools read them directly.

## Test

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/secrets/audit.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:test-types
```

Result: doctor security test passed 1 file / 32 tests; secrets audit test passed 1 file / 24 tests; `git diff --check` passed; `pnpm check:test-types` passed.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` now surfaces plaintext secret-bearing model provider config in `openclaw.json`, including `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout after this patch, running `node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive` against a real temporary config file at `/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/openclaw.json`. The config contained redacted plaintext values in `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_HOME=/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/home \
node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real CLI command above included this live output excerpt:

```text
◇  Security

  - WARNING: openclaw.json contains plaintext secret-bearing config
    fields.
    Paths: models.providers.openai.apiKey,
    models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization
    Agents or workspace tools that can read config files may see these
    API keys/tokens.
    Migrate them to SecretRefs with openclaw secrets configure or
    openclaw secrets apply, then verify with openclaw secrets audit
    --check.
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live doctor run printed a Security warning for both plaintext config paths: `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`, and it pointed the user to SecretRefs plus `openclaw secrets audit --check`.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

Refs #11829

target.title: Feat/browser screenshot vision
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When the main model is text-only (e.g. `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview`), browser screenshots return image content blocks that the model cannot read — the agent sees `[image omitted]` and cannot reason about the page.
- Why it matters: Browser-use workflows are unusable with text-only main models; operators must switch to an expensive multimodal main model just to get screenshot readback.
- What changed:
  - The browser screenshot action now optionally runs the captured image through a configured vision model and returns a text description instead of an image block. This is controlled by new fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, etc.). When unconfigured, behavior is unchanged.
  - Vision descriptions are wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` (same as snapshot/tabs results) to mitigate prompt injection from untrusted web page content.
  - Local screenshot file size is checked against `maxBytes` before sending to the vision provider.
  - `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` in `src/media-understanding/runtime-types.ts` gains `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields, forwarded through `describeImageFileWithModel` to `describeImage` — enabling auth profile selection for all callers of this API, not just the browser tool.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the agent loop, replay-history, session persistence, transport layer, or any other tool. The vision call is entirely contained within the browser extension's screenshot action. The existing `tools.media.image` config and `image_describe` tool are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: browser-use with text-only models
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts` (20 tests)
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `isBrowserVisionEnabled` returns true/false based on config presence, `enabled: false`, empty models, missing provider/model, CLI-type entries
  - `describeBrowserImageWithVision` calls through to `describeImageFileWithModel` with correct params including prompt, timeout, profile, preferredProfile
  - Local file paths as `mediaUrl` are not forwarded (only HTTP(S) URLs)
  - HTTP(S) `mediaUrl` is correctly forwarded
  - Fallback to next candidate when primary fails
  - Empty model response treated as failure and falls through
  - All candidates failing produces aggregate error with per-candidate details
  - `maxChars` truncation with `[truncated]` marker
  - `maxBytes` enforcement via file stat before vision call
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarded to `describeImageFileWithModel`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The helper is a pure function with injected deps — unit tests cover all branches without needing a real browser or vision API.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `browser-tool.test.ts` mock infrastructure extended to include `describeImageFileWithModel` dep injection.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config fields on the top-level `browser` namespace for vision model configuration:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```
  Supported fields: `models[]` (with `provider`, `model`, `prompt`, `maxChars`, `maxBytes`, `timeoutSeconds`, `profile`, `preferredProfile`), `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`.
- When `browser.models` contains at least one valid provider/model entry and `visionEnabled` is not `false`, every browser screenshot is automatically described by the configured vision model. The tool returns a text content block (`[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]\n<wrapped description>`) instead of an image content block. The description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` to guard against prompt injection.
- When `browser.models` is **not** configured, behavior is identical to before — screenshots return image content blocks.
- Set `visionEnabled: false` to force-disable vision without removing the model list.
- If vision description fails (API error, timeout, empty response, file exceeds `maxBytes`), the tool falls back to returning the original image block plus a warning text block so the agent loop can still recover.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` now accepts optional `profile`/`preferredProfile` fields. This is a backward-compatible additive change to the media-understanding runtime contract. All existing callers are unaffected; new callers (like browser vision) can pass auth profile selection.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (text-only main model):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block
    -> transport replaces with "[image omitted]"
    -> agent cannot reason about page

After (browser.models configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool captures screenshot to disk
    -> stat file size → check maxBytes
    -> tool calls vision model (describeImageFileWithModel)
    -> wrapExternalContent(description)
    -> tool returns wrapped text description
    -> text-only main model reads description normally

After (browser.models NOT configured):
  agent calls browser screenshot
    -> tool returns image content block (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — reuses existing `describeImageFileWithModel` which handles auth through the standard provider registry, SecretRef mechanism, and (now) optional auth profiles.
- New/changed network calls? Yes — when `browser.models` is configured, the browser tool makes an additional API call to the configured vision provider to describe the screenshot.
  - Risk: The screenshot image is sent to the configured vision provider's API endpoint.
  - Mitigation: This is opt-in (only when operator explicitly configures `browser.models`). The image data flow is identical to `tools.media.image` / `image_describe` tool — same `describeImageFileWithModel` pipeline, same auth, same SSRF guards.
  - Risk: Vision model descriptions contain untrusted web page content that could include prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: All vision description text is wrapped with `wrapExternalContent(text, { source: "browser", includeWarning: true })`, matching the security treatment applied to snapshot and tabs results.
  - Risk: Raw screenshot could be auto-delivered to chat channels if exposed as deliverable media (related: #44759).
  - Mitigation: Addressed — the vision-success path does not include any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. The raw screenshot file remains on disk for diagnostic purposes only but is not advertised as deliverable media, preventing auto-send of sensitive page content to chat channels.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (darwin)
- Runtime: Node 22, OpenClaw dev build from source (npm link)
- Model/provider: Main model `tencent-tokenhub/hy3-preview` (text-only); vision model `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
- Integration/channel: Control UI webchat
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "browser": {
      "models": [{ "provider": "bytedance", "model": "doubao-seed-2.0-pro" }]
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure `browser.models` with a vision-capable provider/model
2. Start gateway, open dashboard
3. In a new session, ask the agent to take a browser screenshot (e.g. "open https://example.com and take a screenshot")

### Expected

- Agent receives a text description of the screenshot instead of an image block
- Text-only main model can read and reason about the page content
- Tool result includes `[analyzed by <provider>/<model>]` attribution
- Description text is wrapped in external content markers

### Actual

- Confirmed working: agent received text description, main model reasoned about page content normally

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: Gateway log shows `[browser/vision] described screenshot via bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro (chars=NNN)` on successful vision call
- [x] Failing before + passing after: Before config, agent sees `[image omitted]`; after config, agent reads full page description
- [x] Unit tests: 20 tests passing in `extensions/browser/src/browser/vision.test.ts`
- [x] CI: `pnpm check:changed` passes (typecheck core/extensions/tests + lint core/extensions + all guards)
- [x] Screenshot/recording

### Video: Vision model describes screenshot for text-only agent

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5dc315ef-033a-4bee-b13b-e2a8c66e3d4f

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Text-only main models can now read and reason about browser screenshots via a configured vision model, instead of receiving `[image omitted]`.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.3 arm64, Node v25.8.1, OpenClaw built from source (`pnpm build` → `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`), main model hy3-preview (tencent-tokenhub, text-only), vision model doubao-seed-2.0-pro (bytedance), webchat dashboard.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built source: `pnpm build`
  2. Started gateway from source: `node dist/index.js gateway --port 18789`
  3. Configured `browser.models` with `bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
  4. Opened webchat dashboard, asked agent to screenshot a Discord page
  5. Agent captured screenshot → vision model described it → agent reasoned about page content and responded with a text summary
- Evidence after fix: Screen recording showing the full flow: agent takes screenshot, vision model processes it, agent returns a coherent text description of the page content. See "Video: After" above.
- Observed result after fix: Tool result contains `[analyzed by bytedance/doubao-seed-2.0-pro]` attribution + wrapped text description. Main model (hy3-preview) successfully reads and reasons about the Discord channel content.
- What was not tested: Remote/node browser proxy screenshots; multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate tested live); per-agent auth profile selection.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Text-only main model + browser screenshot with `browser.models` configured → text description returned, agent reasons about page
  - Vision call failure → fallback to image block + warning text
  - No `browser.models` configured → original image block behavior unchanged
- Edge cases checked:
  - Local file path passed as `mediaUrl` — fixed: only forward HTTP(S) URLs to describeImageFileWithModel
  - Provider mismatch (`agent-plan` vs `bytedance`) — learned that provider id must match the media-understanding registry, same as `tools.media.image`
  - `wrapExternalContent` applied to vision output — verified in code review
  - `maxBytes` file stat guard — verified via unit test
  - `profile`/`preferredProfile` forwarding — verified end-to-end through runtime-types → runtime.ts → describeImage
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Remote/node browser proxy screenshots (only tested host browser)
  - Multiple fallback candidates in sequence (only single-candidate config tested live; unit test covers this path)
  - Per-agent auth profile selection (see Known Limitations)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

### ClawSweeper findings addressed

- **[P1] Do not advertise vision screenshots as deliverable media** — Fixed: the vision-success path no longer includes any `MEDIA:` directive or `details.media` in the tool result. Raw screenshots are not auto-delivered to chat channels. Related: #44759.
- **[P2] Remove the unused schema import** — Fixed: removed orphaned `ToolsMediaUnderstandingSchema` import from `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — no behavior change without explicit `browser.models` config.
- Config/env changes? Yes — new optional fields on the top-level `browser` config namespace (`models`, `visionEnabled`, `visionPrompt`, `visionMaxChars`, `visionMaxBytes`, `visionTimeoutSeconds`).
- Migration needed? No — purely additive.
- `DescribeImageFileWithModelParams` gains two optional fields (`profile`, `preferredProfile`) — backward compatible, no existing callers affected.

## Known Limitations

- **scope/attachments fields not implemented**: The `tools.media.image` config shape includes `scope` and `attachments` fields, but these are not exposed for `browser` because the browser tool's vision is always triggered on the just-captured screenshot — there is no attachment selection or scope gating semantic. These can be added in a follow-up if a use case emerges.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Vision API latency adds to screenshot tool response time (typically 5–25s depending on provider).
  - Mitigation: Configurable `timeoutSeconds`; on failure, falls back to returning the raw image block so the agent loop is never blocked.
- Risk: Operator configures wrong provider id (e.g. `agent-plan` instead of `bytedance`) and gets silent failures.
  - Mitigation: Error message surfaces the provider/model that failed; the config shape mirrors `tools.media.image` so operators can copy working config directly.
- Risk: Oversized screenshots sent to vision providers.
  - Mitigation: File size checked against `maxBytes` (default 10 MB) before vision call; oversized files skip the candidate with a logged warning.
- Risk: Web page content in vision descriptions could contain prompt injection.
  - Mitigation: Vision output wrapped with `wrapExternalContent` with warning, matching existing snapshot/tabs treatment.

target.title: feat(mattermost): add edit and delete message actions
target.body:
## Summary Adds `patchMattermostPost()` and `deleteMattermostPost()` to the Mattermost client, and wires them as `edit` and `delete` actions in the channel plugin message action adapter. ## What this enables - **Edit messages** via the message tool (`action=edit`) — foundation for block-streaming edit-in-place (progressive message rendering like Telegram/Discord) - **Delete messages** via the message tool (`action=delete`) ## API endpoints used - Edit: `PUT /api/v4/posts/{post_id}/patch` (partial update) - Delete: `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{post_id}` ## Files changed - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts` — add `patchMattermostPost()` and `deleteMattermostPost()` - `extensions/mattermost/src/channel.ts` — register `edit` and `delete` in `listActions`, `supportsAction`, and `handleAction` ## Testing - Tested against a live Mattermost 10.x instance: create → edit → delete cycle works correctly - Bot requires `edit_post` / `delete_post` permissions (standard bot permissions) ## Notes This is a stepping stone toward full block-streaming support on Mattermost, bringing it closer to feature parity with the Telegram and Discord plugins.

Greptile Summary

Added edit and delete message functionality to the Mattermost plugin by implementing `patchMattermostPost()` and `deleteMattermostPost()` in the client, and wiring them through the channel message action adapter. **Changes:** - Added `patchMattermostPost()` function supporting partial updates (message, props, fileIds) - Added `deleteMattermostPost()` function for post deletion - Registered `edit` and `delete` actions in `listActions()` and `supportsAction()` - Implemented action handlers that validate params, resolve account config, and call the API functions - Used standard Mattermost API endpoints (`PUT /posts/{id}/patch`, `DELETE /posts/{id}`) **Architecture:** - Follows existing pattern from `react` action handler (account resolution, validation, error handling) - Accepts both `messageId` and `postId` parameter names for flexibility - Returns structured results with content and details The implementation is straightforward and brings Mattermost closer to feature parity with Telegram and Discord plugins.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge with low risk - Clean implementation following established patterns. The code correctly validates required parameters, handles account resolution, and uses proper error handling. One minor style suggestion about empty message validation, but this won't cause runtime errors since the Mattermost API will reject empty edits if they're not allowed. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: e3df5fa (2/5) Greptile learns from your feedback when you react with thumbs up/down!

target.title: fix(tools): sanitize LLM sentinel tokens in bash exec and web-fetch URL inputs
target.body:
## Summary

Related patches that defend OpenClaw against LLM sentinel-token leakage in tool-call arguments. Smaller models (Gemma 4 26B in our deployment) occasionally emit `<|...|>`, `<|<|"`, `<<|"|`, and similar special tokens at the head of `bash.exec` `command`, `web-fetch` `url`, or file-tool `path`/`file_path` arguments. Without sanitization these flow into `bash -c '<|<|"grep ...'`, `fetch('<|"https://...')`, or `read({file_path: '<<|"|https://...'})`, producing syntax/parse/`ENOENT` errors and forcing the model to hallucinate an answer.

All patches:
- **No-op for clean input** — zero impact on Claude (Opus/Sonnet), Mistral, Llama, or any model that does not leak tokens.
- **Logged** — when sanitize changes the input, an event (`bash.sanitize_special_tokens` / `webfetch.sanitize_special_tokens` / `tool.sanitize_special_tokens`) is emitted for monitoring.

## Commits

| commit | scope | files |
|--------|-------|-------|
| `7cbc14d38` | web-fetch URL sanitize | sanitize-url.ts/test, web-fetch.ts, fetch-guard.ts |
| `fe897846b` | bash exec command sanitize | sanitize-command.ts/test (14 cases), bash-tools.exec.ts |
| `d1265be6b` | `<<` sentinel variants + file-tool path args | sanitize-command.ts/test (22 cases), sanitize-url.ts, sanitize-tool-args.ts/test (9 cases), pi-tools.params.ts |

The bash patch mirrors the URL patch's structure exactly (same predicate set, same trim semantics, same log shape). The follow-up commit `d1265be6b` reuses the same sentinel-stripping library for file-tool path args via the single `normalizeToolParams` chokepoint (read/write/edit and the workspace-root / memory-flush wrappers all route through it), and content-bearing args (`content`/`oldText`/`newText`) are intentionally left untouched so file writes are never mutated.

## Compatibility

Sanitize functions are pure and side-effect-free outside the log emission. Untouched on clean input; safe to land before any regression test runs against the broader exec/fetch/file pipelines. False-positive guards keep a lone leading `<x>` (HTML-like text), a lone leading `"` quote, and bash heredocs (`cat <<EOF`) intact — only token-like leading runs (`>= 2` chars containing `<`) and the `<<|...|` mid-command form are stripped.

## Co-authored

Drafted with Claude Opus 4.7 assistance.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Smaller models — specifically Gemma 4 26B NVFP4 in our deployment — leak LLM sentinel tokens (`<|...|>`, `<|<|"`, `<|"`, `<<|"|`, etc.) at the head of `bash.exec` `command` arguments, `web-fetch` `url` arguments, and file-tool `path`/`file_path` arguments. Without sanitization, the shell receives a command like `<|"|grep something` and returns `bash: syntax error near unexpected token '<|'`. The agent then has no useful tool output and proceeds to fabricate an answer — hallucination caused by silent tool-side failure rather than by model-side reasoning error. The identical failure mode appears on the URL side: `fetch('<|"https://example.com/...')` parses as an invalid URL; and on the file-tool side: `read({file_path: '<<|"|https://...'})` resolves to `ENOENT .../workspace/<|<|` and the agent fabricates file content.

**Real environment tested**: spark-home (DGX Spark GB10, ARM64 Ubuntu 24, 128 GB unified memory). OpenClaw runs under PM2 as a long-lived process via a wrapper script (no `pm2 restart`; reload is `pm2 stop` + `pm2 start` of the wrapper to preserve env). The model backend is Gemma 4 26B NVFP4 served by a local vLLM container on `http://127.0.0.1:8005`. Real Telegram-channel user sessions, not unit fixtures, with the agent named `gemma` (`@lisyoen_gemma_bot`).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Built OpenClaw locally on the branch containing `7cbc14d38`, `fe897846b`, and the follow-up `d1265be6b`.
2. Reloaded OpenClaw via the wrapper (`pm2 stop openclaw && pm2 start openclaw`, wrapper as `pm_exec_path`) to load the patched dist. New gateway PID, port 9086 LISTEN, stable uptime confirmed.
3. Issued real user prompts to the Gemma agent over Telegram that previously caused hallucinated answers — the same prompts that earlier produced `<|"|grep ...` as the bash `command` and `<<|"|https://...` as a `read` `file_path`.
4. Tailed live console output from the running openclaw process: `pm2 logs openclaw --lines 200 --nostream | grep -E 'sanitize_special_tokens'`
5. Compared the new agent answers against the on-disk files to verify content match (no fabrication).

**Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs captured from the live `openclaw` PM2 process on spark-home (KST = UTC+9, command bodies redacted to length-only because they contain user data; the trimmed delta is what the patch removes):

```
2026-05-17 08:13:19  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=6    sanitized_len=1
2026-05-17 08:13:20  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=61   sanitized_len=56
2026-05-17 08:13:22  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=61   sanitized_len=56
2026-05-17 08:13:23  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=42   sanitized_len=37
2026-05-17 08:13:35  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=42   sanitized_len=37
2026-05-17 08:21:33  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=121  sanitized_len=116
2026-05-17 08:21:35  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=73   sanitized_len=68
2026-05-17 08:45:45  bash.sanitize_special_tokens  original_len=73   sanitized_len=68
```

Each line above is a real Gemma 4 26B tool-call. The trimmed delta is consistently around 5 bytes, matching the design (leading `<|...|>` prefix strip with no false positives on the remainder of the command). Forensic artifacts retained on the deployment and available on request (contain user data so not attached here): the quarantined pre-fix session jsonl at `~/.openclaw/agents/gemma/sessions/.quarantine-fresh-20260517-081701/cb03f312-6f71-4a36-a8c2-a16736300294.jsonl` and the pre-fix `sessions.json` backup containing the leaking `agent:gemma:*` mappings.

**Observed result after fix**: The shell no longer receives `<|...|>` prefixed commands. Every `bash.sanitize_special_tokens` event in the runtime log above corresponds to a leak that was successfully trimmed before reaching `bash -c`; the resulting commands execute cleanly. The same Telegram prompts that produced fabricated answers approximately 24 hours before the fix now return the actual on-disk file contents quoted verbatim. Side-by-side comparison confirmed accuracy: every key fact from the pre-fix fabricated answer was invented; every key fact from the post-fix answer matches the on-disk card. Clean commands (no sentinel prefix, the dominant case under Opus/Sonnet sessions) pass through untouched and emit no event, confirming the no-op-for-clean-input design.

**Additional pattern observed 2026-05-17 11:13 KST (follow-up commit `d1265be6b`)**: A smaller-model leak of the form `<<|"|https://...` (double-`<` prefix, no closing `|>`) bypassed the original `<\|[^|>]*\|>` regex, and the leak landed in a `read` tool `file_path` argument — a path not previously sanitized (only `bash.exec` and `web-fetch` URL were). The quarantined session shows `read({file_path: "<<|\"|https://..."})` resolving to `ENOENT, access '.../workspace/<|<|'`, ten consecutive failed toolResults, and the model then emitting a fabricated "success" reply. The follow-up commit extends the regex to strip token-like leading `<`+ runs (`<<|"|`, `<<|`, `<<`, `<<<`) and the mid-command `<<|...|` form, and applies the same sentinel-stripping to file-tool `path`/`file_path` args through the shared `normalizeToolParams` chokepoint (read/write/edit). Unit coverage grew from 14 to 22 sanitize-command cases plus 9 new sanitize-tool-args cases (38 sanitize tests total, all green), including explicit false-positive guards (`<x>` and a lone leading `"` are preserved). After reload, the new `tool.sanitize_special_tokens` event fires for the file-tool path leaks and the `read` call resolves the real path. Forensic jsonl preserved at `~/.openclaw/agents/gemma/sessions/.quarantine-hallucination-20260517-1113/663a8752-71d0-4826-aaed-a9777f741757.jsonl`.

**What was not tested**: Behavior under non-Gemma frontier models that emit similar prefixes — Opus and Sonnet in our deployment did not exhibit this leakage at the moment of testing, so we could not produce a positive sanitize event for them; the patches are no-op for them by design and that no-op behavior is exercised by the included unit tests. Sanitize event rates under heavy concurrent multi-agent load were also not exercised on spark-home where typical concurrency is at most 3. A live Telegram round-trip on the exact 11:13 prompt after the follow-up reload is pending the operator's own Telegram client (the gateway-side path sanitize and unit suite are verified).
